item.title: Wikiwalk Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show Proposed edit
- Hide

To link to another skip about roman history, I propose the following edit:

the common language used during early Imperial Rome

to

the common language used during early Imperial Rome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh fuck yes. Obscure historical trivia? check. Enduring mystery examined? check. Intelligent use of humor? check.

I absolutely adore this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Re: last paragraph. I took it to mean that between events, any one member of the legion may appear at any of the three locations, but the legion itself may be all three (say, X soldiers in Scotland and Y in Armenia) but during one event one specific soldier can only appear in one location, no repeats. It's referred to a second time when mentioning the commander's appearances.

Congratulations and stuff.
I love a well-done Roman Empire skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote for sure from me: I am no history buff, but I have heard of the Lost Legion, and I think the addenda help trigger the *wham* moment of realization. In the fictional world of the Foundation, this actually makes the MOST sense (to me) of what happened to the legion.

Maybe add a tease to what could happen if the Legion is not contained? Right now it feels like they would be too shell-shocked to really go rampaging and so are not that "dangerous", not that "dangerous" makes a good scip. I guess, justification for Euclid class, beyond them being sapient. Could be a personal thing.

Finally - "SCP-2012 instances will tend to be scattered, and can appear in multiple locations during one manifestation event, but instances are never repeated in the different locations in which SCP-2012 can manifest." I am having a little trouble parsing exactly what that means. I read it as the Legion may appear in Scotland and Armenia at the same time, but never Scotland twice during one event. Is that accurate? Some clarification may be beneficial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: he knew a guy who knew a guy who knew a guy who knew that the aqualifer's brother was in Britain? lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same diety, just a latinized spelling of the name. His Roman counterpart was Vejovis, but Asclepius/Aesculapius was actually the more widely followed diety, even toward the end of the Roman empire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't understand 1 thing: Asclepius is the Greece's god, in Rome - Aesculap. Is that an author's mistake or what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the premise of Jim Butcher's Codex Alera.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had to stop reading after the first praragraph of the description.

Because I had to immediately a) wiki IX Hispania and b) thren *immediately* go to the timeline for my fantasy RPG campaign world with pseudo-Romans and add an "IX Legion disappears without trace" incident to the timeline, because the idea of a whole Roman Legion up and vanishing is SO COOL. (And despite my pretty good knowledge of Romans, I'd never heard of this one before!)

So, you pretty much had me on the concept alone by that point, but the rest was great.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The line, "It's some other emperor I don't even recognize" made me upvote. That opened it up to who knows what. Is this a reference to something that exists elsewhere? This line grabbed my imagination so strongly I couldn't not upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article is a breath of fresh air from most roman themed SCPs, but I feel that there was an incredible amount of things that could be done with the story. Unfortunately the story didn't go anywhere, it was just the lost legion, but it was written well enough warrant an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why, you've tickled my history bone quite expertly. Have a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The problem, I think, is that the "story" of this SCP is the story of the missing legion, which most people probably aren't aware of. I wasn't anyway, until I read the discussion. With that knowledge this is a neat idea—without it, it's just "so what?".

I agree with ScienceBoink about the equipment—period materials and appearance should be sufficient. I see no need for them to be made with period techniques.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cyntax=Spot on

*syntax, and background is one word.

Not all SCPs need to have a 1000 word long story. Sometimes what suffices can be described in one short sentence. There are a few recent ones that are more mysterious and leave the author with more to think about.

Tl;dr I wouldn't say that there's no story element here. It just happens to leave more to the author's imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tone=Marvelous
Cyntax=Spot on
mystery=nailed it.

But the story element, that's supposed to be in all scips in way or the other is somehow gone. It's essentially a time traveling lost army that has no idea the hell is going on. I like the historical back-ground, but the hook is too weak to warrant a strong, good upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say that I found myself curious by the name, but in the end, I was a little disappointed. Granted, I don't find it worthy for a downvote, but I feel like you gave too much information in some places and not enough in others. A good example is your explanation of Alphas 1-3. I feel like you could shorten their explanation into a single paragraph without losing any info you have put in. Overall, I will say you have painted a good image here, and I feel that it's missing just that little something extra that made me want to upvote and come back to read again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but it has a couple things that just bug me.

Vulgar Latin. Is it possible to learn Vulgar Latin? As I understand it, the Latin normally taught in schools is church Latin, aka Latin after a bunch of changes because it stopped being a living language. Do we still know what V.L. sounded like? Also, would there be regional/time differences that would mean the three Agents would need to learn different versions?

Equipment: Wouldn't it be quicker to make using modern methods, and then 'rough it up' in some way to make it look authentic? The agent shouldn't be taking it off and passing it around for examination after all. Also, are there any equipment differences between the time periods?

Why don't we keep the agent on-site full time, in case the SCP appears at a different time of year?

2012-1's name shouldn't be blacked out in the containment procedure. The whole point is to blend in and act Roman to keep the SCP contained. You need to know the commander's name so you can greet him by name. Walking up to a legion commander and saying "Ave, 2012-1" is a bad thing.

No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Remember the remembrances that are being remembered by the members of the remembering members of the remembered legion?

But seriously though, the ending note of the addendum is important because it helps establish the hint that the legion is

SPOILER ALERT
AMGAD

temporally displaced. They're stuck in time, and are experiencing a series of different events all at the same time. That's why the commander remembers fighting a war that happens in the future. It sounds a little repetitive, but I had to throw it in there because it would be a little too subtle otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm on the fence here. The end Addendum just falls flat for me. I'm not sure if I missed something, but it just seems to repeat itself again and again there. Specifically:

SCP-2012-1: And you know what's the strangest thing? I couldn't stop seeing those Jewish zealots, and thinking of Parthian foot infantry. I've never seen Parthian foot infantry, but when I saw those soldiers, I couldn't stop thinking of how much they reminded me of the old Parthian wars. See, there I go again. I have no experience in the Parthian wars, but they seem to be old wars to me. We haven't even fought the Parthians since Trajan, but in those memories, it's not Trajan that's flying on those standards. It's some other emperor that I don't even recognize.

By the time I was done reading that, I wanted to yell, "I get it, I get it! You don't remember fighting them, but you remember fighting them. It's like you remember something that didn't happen, wonderful!"

Also, how did they try to discover the origin of SCP-2012 exactly? All we have are clips of SCP-2012-1 remembering things he doesn't remember remembering to remember remembering members remembered. Remember?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have liked to know some details about what the legion does while it is manifested, but otherwise I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It looks like the SCP-2012 slot finally has an article that stuck.

In general, I like it. Solid presentation, reasonably interesting, and weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot. I don't feel it's missing anything, and I think extra stuff runs the risk of feeling extraneous.

One thing, though: personnel is a plural noun. You cannot have 'a personnel'. I suggest replacing with 'a member of staff'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry I couldn't give you a more constructive criticism in the chat other than "I like it". But I liked it back then and I like this improved version now, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Britain is certainly the most likely place that the legion could have been destroyed at, but the point of my suggestion in this particular case was to use the ambiguity about the legion's fate among historians as the hook for this particular SCP, hence its appearance in various places the legion might have been involved in. The Lost Legion has been a constant point of contention and confusion among historians, which is why I thought that the same confusion existing among the members of the legion itself would make a neat hook. I think that it is, hence my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the same thing. I consulted with Dmatic though, the resident Roman history buff, who's actually studying for a bachelor's in history with a focus on Roman history. He said that the ultimate fate of the Lost Legion is currently unknown, but that there were some hypotheses about their fate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well written, an interesting hook… Good skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the Roman Empire/Republic
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suspect this would have flown a couple years ago, but after 2000+ articles, my reaction is "Roman ghost legion…okay, and…?"

The length demands a better payoff, and the subject isn't weird and/or interesting enough to carry the article just by showing up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see it now…

"SCP-XXXX: 'Gurren Legion'"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? I could've sworn they went missing/were destroyed whilst being stationed in Britain. I mean since it was so long ago it's possible that they were at any of those places when they disappeared, but I'm really, REALLY sure that I read somewhere that some guy knew some guys who said that they had a friend who knew that the aqualifer's brother was in Britain, but I dunno.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As one who knows Latin and loves Roman history, I couldn't help but to upvote. +1 (barely though for the same reasons as above)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thirded.

I had considered writing about the Lost Legion once. The plan involved mecha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, I got the exact same feeling from this. I love the concept, but nothing about the execution made me want to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The places themselves are actually all hypothesized areas in which Legio IX Hispana could have potentially been lost in combat.

EDIT: Oh crap, this was supposed to be a reply to JARZ. Oh well

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little iffy on this one, leaning more towards an upvote.

I like the concept and when I saw "IX Hispana" I couldn't help but fanboy a bit. With that said, I couldn't help but feel as if something was lacking from this article. The tone seems fine, and the concept's pretty cool (the missing 9th Legion showing up in random places), but I just don't know whether or not I like it. No vote for now, but perhaps I'll warm up to it after a third reading.

PS: I'm pretty sure it's spelled "Aesculapius," but I have seen the spelling you've used somewhere, I suppose.

item.title: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
This was inspired from my desire to have more Roman-centric articles on the site, and my love of historical SCP's.

Thanks to Dmatix especially for this long and patient critique in which he kindly pointed out how much I didn't understand Roman history, and how I could reshape this SCP to be interesting.

Other thanks go out to the various members of chat who reviewed the original version of the draft, and who said that they enjoyed it anyways.
